Party_Details.Name: Cleantech Partners Hangzhou Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Cleantech Partners Hangzhou Pte Ltd (CTPHZ) is a wholly-owned subsidiary of Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd (CTP).

On 31 May 2010, Han signed a collaboration agreement with Vanwarm on CTPHZ's behalf. Under the agreement, a joint venture company named Hangzhou Vanwarm Cleantech Co Ltd (HVC) was to be set up, and Vanwarm undertook to arrange for Hong Kong Hong Jia Investments Co Ltd to lend CTPHZ USD6 million for CTPHZ's 40% share of HVC's registered capital.
On 17 June 2010, HVC was incorporated and CTPHZ appointed Han and Robin to HVC's board.
On 12 October 2010, Han and Christine were removed from the board of CTPHZ.

On 31 January 2011, HQEDA informed CTP and Vanwarm that HVC's registered capital was not in place by the agreed deadline of 31 December 2010 and that construction work in the Hangzhou project had not started by the end of 2010, and it extended the relevant deadlines.
By the extended deadline of 10 March 2011, the requisite registered capital for HVC had not been injected.
On 11 March 2011, HQEDA informed CTP and Vanwarm that the Tripartite Agreement had been terminated.
On 31 March 2011, Vanwarm terminated the Collaboration Agreement on the ground that HQEDA had terminated the Tripartite Agreement.
On 3 April 2011, Hong Jia terminated the Loan Agreement.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the defendants' alleged breaches of duty or conspiracy caused CTPHZ to lose the Hangzhou project or diverted a corporate opportunity from CTPHZ.
Party_Details.Rule: A plaintiff alleging breach of fiduciary duty or conspiracy must prove the material facts of the alleged misconduct and that the misconduct caused the pleaded loss. Where diversion of a corporate opportunity is alleged, the plaintiff must show that the opportunity belonged to the plaintiff and was in fact diverted.
Party_Details.Application: CTPHZ participated in the Hangzhou project through agreements with Vanwarm and HQEDA and through HVC. Han and Robin were appointed to HVC's board, and Han and Christine were later removed from CTPHZ's board in October 2010. After that removal, the deadlines for capital injection and commencement of the project were extended and later the underlying agreements were terminated. CTPHZ alleges that the defendants' conduct after the internal dispute caused the loss of the project and involved the diversion of opportunities to IEC.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that the plaintiffs' loss of the Hangzhou project was entirely self-inflicted. By October 2010, the plaintiffs had aggressively ousted Dr. Han and his associates from the CTPHZ board. Having removed the defendants from corporate control, the plaintiffs assumed full responsibility for meeting the capital injection deadlines set by the Chinese authorities. The plaintiffs' failure to inject the funds by March 2011 triggered the termination of the joint venture. The court concluded there was zero causal link between the defendants' post-ouster conduct and the termination of the agreements. Furthermore, the allegations that the defendants conspired to sabotage the project or successfully diverted it to their new company (IEC) were entirely speculative, lacking any concrete evidence of actual diversion or Japanese investment resulting from the defendants' actions. Therefore, the conspiracy and breach of duty claims were dismissed in their entirety.

Party_Details.Name: Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd (CTP) is a Singapore company whose founder directors included Patrick Teo, Richard Lim, Michael Heng and Robin.
In late 2009, CTP collaborated with Hangzhou Vanwarm Holdings Group Ltd to develop the Hangzhou Singapore Eco-Park development project in Hangzhou, China.

In early March 2010, CTP's founder directors, Han Cheng Fong, Christine and Cleantech Ventures Asia Pte Ltd signed a document dated 1 March 2010 recording arrangements relating to share allocations, directorships, Han's role in a new subsidiary for the Hangzhou project, and a profit-sharing structure between CTP and that subsidiary.

On 23 March 2010, CTP entered into a tripartite agreement with Hangzhou Qianjiang Economic Development Area Management Committee and Hangzhou Vanwarm Holdings Group Ltd to collaborate on the preparation of a master plan for, and the development and promotion of, the Hangzhou project.
On 7 April 2010, Han informed CTP's directors that he would focus on CTPHZ and not sit on CTP's board so as not to be involved in CTP's fund-raising efforts.

On 10 May 2010, Takeshi Fujimoto of the Japan Export Trade Organisation emailed Richard Lim and Patrick Teo stating that the Japanese Ministry of Trade and Industry and some Japanese companies were interested in the Hangzhou project.
On 28 July 2010, Robin, who was then a director of CTP and CTPHZ, went to Tokyo for a presentation on the Hangzhou project to METI and potential Japanese investors.
Between June and August 2010, emails were exchanged between Patrick, Richard and others referring to plans to remove Han and Christine from their positions.
By August 2010, Patrick and Richard were trying to sell their CTP shares to a Malaysian company, Bintan Kindenko Corporation Berhad.
On 25 August 2010, Han instructed CTP's company secretary, Ng Chee Tiong, to ask all CTPHZ directors to disclose their directorships.
On 5 September 2010, Patrick Teo emailed the other CTPHZ directors stating that he would address issues raised by Han Cheng Fong with the company secretary in a memo to CTP members at a later date.
On 24 September 2010, Han Cheng Fong arranged for a CTPHZ board meeting to be held on 29 September 2010.
On 27 September 2010, Patrick Teo emailed Richard Lim and Low Soo Chee asking for their support not to attend the upcoming CTPHZ board meeting.

On 1 October 2010, Patrick Teo instructed company secretary Ng Chee Tiong to retain CTPHZ's records until further notice because the majority of CTP's board did not agree to the change of CTPHZ's company secretary.

On 5 October 2010, Han Cheng Fong emailed Patrick Teo stating that he remained committed to the Hangzhou project and had communicated documentary material about Patrick Teo and Richard Lim's conduct to Liu Hai and Vanwarm.
On 21 October 2010, Ng emailed Han the resolutions that dismissed him from his positions in CTPHZ.
On 7 December 2010, Michael sent an email to Liu Hai expressing unhappiness about Liu Hai's visit to Singapore and stating that CTP would terminate its relationship with Vanwarm.
On 10 December 2010, Michael sent another email to Liu Hai that referred to legal action commenced by Han against CTP.
On 15 December 2010, Michael sent a further email to Liu Hai.
On 2 February 2011, Vanwarm terminated the Collaboration Agreement on the ground of internal problems at CTP.
On 11 May 2011, CTP's Chinese solicitors informed HQEDA that CTP had decided to delay the injection of funds for the registered capital of HVC and requested a meeting with HQEDA.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether former directors breached their fiduciary duties by allegedly diverting a joint venture corporate opportunity to a newly formed entity.
Party_Details.Rule: A plaintiff alleging civil conspiracy or breach of fiduciary duty must prove the material acts relied on, the existence of the alleged wrong, and that the alleged wrong caused the plaintiff's loss. A claim based on diversion of corporate opportunity also requires proof that a concrete opportunity of the plaintiff was diverted.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court determined that CTP was the architect of its own misfortune. The evidentiary record clearly showed that CTP's founding directors orchestrated a hostile takeover of the subsidiary's board, effectively neutralizing the defendants' ability to manage the Hangzhou project. Following the ouster, the defendants lacked the authority to authorize the necessary capital injections. CTP's failure to meet the extended funding deadlines—coupled with its directors sending abrasive emails that alienated their Chinese partners—was the sole and direct cause of the joint venture's termination. The court found the allegations that the defendants engineered the termination or conspired to divert the project to their new company to be baseless speculation unsupported by any concrete evidence. As CTP failed to establish causation, the conspiracy and breach of duty claims were entirely dismissed.

Party_Details.Name: Han Cheng Fong

Facts.Text: In late 2009, CTP's founder directors invited Han Cheng Fong to participate in the Hangzhou project.

In early March 2010, Han Cheng Fong, CTP's founder directors and Cleantech Ventures Asia Pte Ltd signed the 1 March document, which provided among other things that Han would become chairman and a director of a new subsidiary of CTP to be set up for the Hangzhou project.
On 1 April 2010, CTPHZ was incorporated and Han became its chairman. The other directors were Robin, Patrick, Richard and Christine.
On 7 April 2010, Han informed CTP's directors that he would focus his attention on CTPHZ and not sit on CTP's board.

On 31 May 2010, Han signed a collaboration agreement with Vanwarm on CTPHZ's behalf. Under the agreement, a joint venture company named Hangzhou Vanwarm Cleantech Co Ltd was to be set up, and Vanwarm undertook to arrange for Hong Kong Hong Jia Investments Co Ltd to lend CTPHZ USD6 million.
On 17 June 2010, HVC was incorporated in China and CTPHZ appointed Han and Robin to HVC's board.
By August 2010, emails between Patrick and Richard about removing Han and Christine from their positions had been passed by Robin to Han and Christine.
On 25 August 2010, Han instructed Ng to ask all CTPHZ directors to disclose their directorships.
On 24 September 2010, Han arranged for a meeting of CTPHZ's board to be held on 29 September 2010.
On 5 October 2010, Han emailed Patrick stating that he remained committed to the Hangzhou project and had communicated documentary material about Patrick and Richard's conduct to Liu Hai and Vanwarm.
On 12 October 2010, Han was removed as a director of CTPHZ and replaced as CTPHZ's corporate representative on HVC.
On 21 October 2010, Ng emailed Han the resolutions that dismissed him from his positions in CTPHZ.
On 18 and 19 November 2010, Han attended meetings in Hangzhou with Vanwarm's representatives and an architect to discuss the master plan for the Hangzhou project.
On 23 November 2010, Han and Robin signed an HVC board resolution that authorised the company's representative to bid for land put up for tender.

From 17 to 19 February 2011, Han went to Tokyo with representatives of Vanwarm and HQEDA to meet representatives from JETRO, METI and Japanese companies regarding investment opportunities in the Qianjiang Economic Development Area.
On 28 June 2011, Han attended the ground-breaking ceremony for the Hangzhou project in Hangzhou as an invited guest of Vanwarm.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the formation of a new company and subsequent business meetings by former directors constitute an unlawful diversion of a corporate opportunity.
Party_Details.Rule: A claim for breach of fiduciary duty or conspiracy requires proof of the material acts relied on and proof that those acts caused the plaintiff's loss. Where a defendant continues to act in another capacity, the court must consider the scope of that role and whether the conduct complained of was inconsistent with duties owed to the plaintiff.
Party_Details.Application: Following their removal from the plaintiffs' boards, former directors incorporated a new entity and engaged in overseas business trips. The dispute centers on whether this new entity usurped the plaintiffs' specific joint venture project, especially in light of the plaintiffs' prior failure to meet capital injection deadlines for that project.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that Dr. Han's continued involvement with the Chinese partners was lawful and did not constitute a breach of duty or conspiracy against CTPHZ. Although ousted from CTPHZ, Dr. Han remained a legally appointed director of the Chinese joint venture entity (HVC). His attendance at the master plan meetings, authorization of the land bid, and participation in the Tokyo trip were consistent with his continuing obligations to HVC. The court noted that attracting Japanese investors to the economic zone would have ultimately benefited HVC, and by extension, CTPHZ as a 40% shareholder. The court wholly rejected the plaintiffs' speculative theory that Dr. Han orchestrated the termination of the project. The sole cause of the joint venture's collapse was the plaintiffs' own failure to inject the required capital by the deadlines set by the Chinese authorities. Because Dr. Han's actions did not cause the loss, and there was no evidence he diverted the project to his own company, the claims against him were dismissed.

Party_Details.Name: Low Soo Chee

Facts.Text: Low Soo Chee (Robin) was one of the founder directors of Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd.

Between June and August 2010, emails were exchanged between Patrick, Richard and others about removing Han and Christine from their positions, and Robin handed copies of certain emails to Han and Christine.
On 28 July 2010, Robin, who was then a director of CTP and CTPHZ, went to Tokyo for a presentation on the Hangzhou project to METI and potential Japanese investors.
On 29 September 2010, Robin attended a CTPHZ board meeting with Han and Christine at which the company secretary and registered address were changed and the board composition was confirmed.
On 12 October 2010, Han and Christine were removed as directors of CTPHZ.
On 11 November 2010, Robin, Christine, Fujimoto and a team of Japanese company representatives visited Hangzhou.
On 18 and 19 November 2010, Robin attended meetings in Hangzhou with Han, Christine and Vanwarm's representatives to discuss the master plan for the Hangzhou project.
On 23 November 2010, Han and Robin signed an HVC board resolution that authorised the company's representative to bid for land put up for tender.

From 17 to 19 February 2011, Robin went to Tokyo with representatives of Vanwarm and HQEDA to meet representatives from JETRO, METI and Japanese companies regarding investment opportunities in the Qianjiang Economic Development Area.
On 28 June 2011, Robin attended the ground-breaking ceremony as Vanwarm's guest.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Robin breached duties owed to the plaintiffs or participated in a conspiracy that caused the plaintiffs to lose the Hangzhou project or diverted an opportunity to IEC.
Party_Details.Rule: A claim for breach of fiduciary duty or conspiracy requires proof of the material acts relied on and proof that those acts caused the plaintiff's loss. Conduct after changes in corporate roles must be assessed in light of the role the defendant still held and whether the conduct was inconsistent with duties owed to the plaintiff.
Party_Details.Application: Robin was a founder director of CTP and also served on the boards of CTPHZ and HVC. He passed certain internal emails to Han and Christine, later attended meetings connected with the Hangzhou project, signed an HVC resolution authorising a land bid, and participated in a Tokyo investment trip. The plaintiffs allege that these acts formed part of a conspiracy and contributed to the loss of the Hangzhou project or the diversion of opportunities to IEC.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found no evidence to support the claims of conspiracy or breach of duty against Robin. His decision to hand over emails detailing a hostile plot against the Chairman was not an actionable conspiracy against the company. Regarding his post-ouster activities, Robin's attendance at the master plan meetings and his authorization of the land bid were legitimate functions of his continuing directorship in the Chinese joint venture (HVC), actions that ultimately stood to benefit CTPHZ as a 40% shareholder. While he attended the Tokyo trip under the banner of his new company (IEC), the court found no proof that this trip diverted the Hangzhou project or resulted in the poaching of any specific Japanese investors away from CTPHZ. Crucially, the plaintiffs failed to prove that any of Robin's actions caused the termination of the joint venture; the termination was entirely the result of the plaintiffs' own failure to meet the capital injection deadlines. Consequently, the claims against Robin were dismissed.

Party_Details.Name: Liew Sok Kuan

Facts.Text: In early March 2010, Liew Sok Kuan signed the 1 March 2010 document together with CTP's founder directors, Han Cheng Fong and Cleantech Ventures Asia Pte Ltd, recording arrangements connected with the Hangzhou project.
On 1 April 2010, upon incorporation of CTPHZ, Liew Sok Kuan became a director of CTPHZ.
On 18 September 2010, Liew Sok Kuan and Low Soo Chee incorporated Green Solutions @ ARB Pte Ltd.
On 20 January 2011, Green Solutions @ ARB Pte Ltd changed its name to International Eco-City Pte Ltd (IEC).

On 29 September 2010, Liew Sok Kuan attended a CTPHZ board meeting with Han Cheng Fong and Low Soo Chee at which the company secretary and registered address were changed and the board composition was confirmed.
On 12 October 2010, Liew Sok Kuan was removed from her position as a director of CTPHZ at an Extraordinary General Meeting.
On 11 November 2010, shortly after her removal from CTPHZ, Liew Sok Kuan visited Hangzhou with Low Soo Chee, Takeshi Fujimoto and a team of Japanese company representatives.

From 17 to 19 February 2011, Liew Sok Kuan travelled to Tokyo with Han Cheng Fong, Low Soo Chee, and representatives of Vanwarm and HQEDA to meet JETRO, METI and Japanese companies regarding investment opportunities in the Qianjiang Economic Development Area.
On 28 June 2011, Liew Sok Kuan attended the ground-breaking ceremony for the Hangzhou project in Hangzhou as an invited guest of Vanwarm.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Liew Sok Kuan breached duties owed to the plaintiffs or participated in a conspiracy that caused the plaintiffs to lose the Hangzhou project or diverted an opportunity to IEC.
Party_Details.Rule: A claim for breach of fiduciary duty or conspiracy requires proof of the material acts relied on and proof that those acts caused the plaintiff's loss. Where a defendant later becomes involved with another company, the plaintiff must still prove an actual diversion of opportunity or other actionable misconduct.
Party_Details.Application: Liew Sok Kuan was involved in the Hangzhou project through CTPHZ, was later removed from CTPHZ's board, and had incorporated Green Solutions @ ARB Pte Ltd with Robin before that removal. The company was later renamed IEC. After her removal from CTPHZ, she continued to attend certain Hangzhou- and Tokyo-related meetings with Han, Robin, and representatives of Vanwarm and HQEDA. The plaintiffs allege that this conduct formed part of a conspiracy and diverted the Hangzhou project or related opportunities to IEC.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court rejected the claims of conspiracy and diversion of corporate opportunity against Christine. The plaintiffs' allegations were based entirely on speculative inferences rather than concrete evidence. There was no proof that Christine's participation in the Tokyo trip or her ongoing interactions with the Chinese partners resulted in any Japanese investment being diverted to IEC, nor was there any evidence that IEC acquired an interest in the Hangzhou project. Furthermore, the court emphasized that the termination of the joint venture was caused exclusively by the plaintiffs' own failure to inject the required capital by the contractual deadlines, breaking any chain of causation linked to Christine's activities. Without proof of actual diversion or a causal link to the plaintiffs' loss, the claims against her were dismissed.

Party_Details.Name: International Eco-City Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: On 2010-09-18, a company named Green Solutions @ ARB Pte Ltd was incorporated in Singapore by Low Soo Chee and Liew Sok Kuan.
On 2011-01-20, Green Solutions @ ARB Pte Ltd changed its name to International Eco-City Pte Ltd (IEC).
By early 2011, Han Cheng Fong, Low Soo Chee and Liew Sok Kuan were directors of International Eco-City Pte Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether IEC received any diverted corporate opportunity from the plaintiffs or participated in a conspiracy that caused the plaintiffs to lose the Hangzhou project.
Party_Details.Rule: A plaintiff alleging diversion of corporate opportunity or conspiracy against a company must prove facts showing that the company actually received the disputed opportunity or otherwise participated in conduct that caused the plaintiff's loss.
Party_Details.Application: Green Solutions @ ARB Pte Ltd was incorporated in September 2010 and was renamed International Eco-City Pte Ltd in January 2011. By early 2011, Han Cheng Fong, Low Soo Chee and Liew Sok Kuan were directors of IEC. The plaintiffs allege that IEC was used as the vehicle through which the Hangzhou project or related investment opportunities were diverted from the plaintiffs.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found the plaintiffs' claims against IEC to be entirely speculative, describing them as an exercise in 'commercial creativity' rather than fact-based litigation. Official Chinese records demonstrated that CTPHZ remained the 40% shareholder of the joint venture company long after IEC's formation; there was absolutely no documentary or transactional evidence showing that IEC had acquired any interest in the Hangzhou project or had replaced CTPHZ. Furthermore, the evidence indicated that IEC's actual business activities involved unrelated products like waterless urinals and paints, and no Japanese company had actually invested in the Hangzhou project as a result of the Tokyo trip. Because the plaintiffs failed to prove that IEC received any diverted opportunity or caused the failure of the joint venture (which failed due to the plaintiffs' own lack of funding), all claims against IEC were dismissed.
